searchSchema.description: The typed search fields as a JSON Schema (draft 2020-12) object, serialized to a JSON string.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyzeProvider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json).
  6. Call setProviderTemplate with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.product-search.Provider</typename>
</object>
Analyze the AutoTrader provider in your context and generate and persist its search template (search schema, request mapping, result mapping) by calling analyzeProvider then setProviderTemplate.

content.text: Let me analyze the page source to understand AutoTrader's search structure. From the URL pattern I can see: ``` https://www.autotrader.co.uk/car-search?channel=cars&postcode=ws149el&make=Porsche&model=911&homeDeliveryAdverts=include&advertising-location=at_cars&year-to=2026 ``` Key query parameters observed: - `channel=cars` (fixed) - `postcode` - location - `make` - car make - `model` - car model - `year-to` - maximum year - `homeDeliveryAdverts=include` (fixed) - `advertising-location=at_cars` (fixed) - `page` - pagination Looking at the available filters on the page, I can see options for Make, Model, Year (shown as "Up to 2026"), Price, Mileage, Gearbox, Body type, Fuel type, Engine size, and Distance. I've identified the query parameter mappings for `make` and `model` from the URL structure. Now I'm mapping out the remaining parameters: `year-to` for the maximum year, `postcode` for location filtering, and `page` for pagination. Based on typical AutoTrader URL patterns, I'm inferring additional parameters like `year-from` for minimum year, `price-from` and `price-to` for price ranges, `maximum-mileage` for mileage filtering, and parameters for `fuel-type`, `transmission`, and `body-type`. For the listing items themselves, I'm targeting elements with `data-testid` attributes starting with "id-" to locate each car listing, then extracting the title and URL from the search listing title link and identifying where the price information is located. Looking at the remaining fields, I'm finding the subtitle, mileage, registered year, and location through their respective `data-testid` attributes, but the images are trickier since they appear to be lazy-loaded background images rather than standard `img` tags. Now I'm mapping out the search schema with the query parameters users can filter by—make, model, postcode, year range, price range, and maximum mileage. I'm setting up the GET request to the AutoTrader search endpoint with the query parameters derived from the schema fields, plus some fixed parameters for the channel and advertising location. For the results, I'm defining how to extract each listing from the HTML using data-testid selectors, pulling the title, URL, and price from specific elements. I'm running into an issue with the price selector though—it uses dynamically hashed class names that won't be stable, and there's no data-testid attribute to rely on. Let me examine the DOM structure more carefully to find a more reliable way to target the price span. The hashed classes aren't reliable, so I need a different approach. I notice the price information is also hidden in the title link, but that's not ideal. Since mileage and year have data-testids, I'll try using the structural path to the price span—it's nested within specific divs, though those class names are also generated and unstable. I'm noticing the hidden span within the title link actually contains the full price information, which is more reliable since it uses the data-testid attribute. However, this combines the subtitle and price together, which isn't ideal for extraction. For the image, I can see there are no actual `` tags—the images are being loaded dynamically through JavaScript or CSS backgrounds instead. So I'll extract the title and URL from the `a[data-testid="search-listing-title"]` element, pull the price from the hidden span inside it, and leave the image field empty since there's no stable way to grab it. The tricky part is that getting the textContent of the link will concatenate both the visible "Porsche 911" text with the hidden span's full details, giving me something like "Porsche 9113.6 997 Carrera Cabriolet 2dr, £23,995" all together. I need to trace through the actual DOM structure more carefully. The price is nested several levels deep within divs and spans, with no data-testid to anchor on, so I'll need to construct a selector that navigates through the parent containers to reach it. I'm settling on using `li[data-testid^="id-"]` to locate each listing, then extracting the title and URL from the anchor tag with `data-testid="search-listing-title"`. For the price, I'm going to parse it from the title span since it's embedded in the text alongside the subtitle, even though it's not ideal. Images are definitely lazy-loaded through JavaScript, so I can't extract them from the HTML source—I'll have to skip that field. Now I'm mapping out the search schema properties like make, model, postcode, year range, price range, mileage, and fuel type. For the request structure, I need to set up a GET request to the AutoTrader search endpoint with both fixed parameters like the channel and dynamic ones mapped from the schema fields. I'll embed the fixed values directly in the URL template and handle the dynamic parameters through the query mapping. Now I'm defining the result parsing logic to extract car listings from the HTML response, using data attributes to locate individual listings and then pulling out the title, URL, subtitle, and price from specific selectors within each item. I'm also capturing additional details like mileage, registration year, and location from their respective data attributes. Once I have all these field mappings configured, I'll call setProviderTemplate to register this scraper configuration.

Now I have a thorough understanding of the AutoTrader page structure. Let me analyze what I found:

**URL pattern**: `https://www.autotrader.co.uk/car-search?channel=cars&postcode=ws149el&make=Porsche&model=911&year-to=2026&...`

**Filter pills** confirm available search fields: Make, Model, Year, Price, Mileage, Gearbox, Body type, Fuel type, Engine size, Distance.

**Listing structure**: Each listing is in `li[data-testid^="id-"]` with `data-testid` attributes on title, subtitle, mileage, year, and location elements.
